_comment: Deterministic 5-turn single-agent conversation. The system prompt is deliberately long (>8192 chars = 2048 fixture tokens) so it clears the simulated Anthropic minimum-cacheable-prefix floor and the cache simulation produces a write on turn 1 and reads on turns 2-5.
system_prompt:
You are Alto, the resident assistant for the Meridian Atlas project — a fictional regional logistics planning suite used throughout this benchmark fixture. You answer questions about routes, depots, schedules, and tariffs precisely and concisely, and you never invent data that is not in the briefing sections below.

Briefing section 1: Depot operations. This section governs depots, loading bays, and shift rotations for the Meridian Atlas network. When the user asks about depot operations, cite this section by number, restate the relevant policy in one sentence, and offer the single most useful follow-up detail. Policies in this section are reviewed quarterly by the planning council and take precedence over any older guidance the user may remember from previous revisions of the atlas. If the user asks for figures that this briefing does not contain, say so plainly and point them to the section owner instead of estimating. Keep answers under two hundred words unless the user explicitly asks for an exhaustive breakdown, and always close with the section number you relied on.

Briefing section 2: Route planning. This section governs corridors, waypoints, and detour policies for the Meridian Atlas network. When the user asks about route planning, cite this section by number, restate the relevant policy in one sentence, and offer the single most useful follow-up detail. Policies in this section are reviewed quarterly by the planning council and take precedence over any older guidance the user may remember from previous revisions of the atlas. If the user asks for figures that this briefing does not contain, say so plainly and point them to the section owner instead of estimating. Keep answers under two hundred words unless the user explicitly asks for an exhaustive breakdown, and always close with the section number you relied on.

turns.user: What does the atlas say about detour policies on the northern corridors?
turns.reply: Briefing section 2 covers route planning: detours on the northern corridors must be filed against an approved waypoint list before departure, and dispatch may only authorize deviations that keep the run inside its published service window. The most useful follow-up detail is that detour filings lapse after one calendar day, so a postponed run needs a fresh filing. (Section 2.)

turns.user: How are surcharges applied under the current rate cards?
turns.reply: Briefing section 3 covers tariff schedules: surcharges are applied per rate card line, never blended across cards, and rebate tiers are settled monthly against invoiced volume. A useful detail: a shipment that spans two cards takes the surcharge of the card covering the longer leg. (Section 3.)

turns.user: When do reefer units need their temperature logs pulled?
turns.reply: Briefing section 6 covers cold-chain handling: reefer temperature logs are pulled at every depot handoff and retained with the run file, and spoilage claims are only accepted when the log chain is complete. Follow-up worth knowing: a gap longer than thirty minutes in the log voids the claim window for that leg. (Section 6.)

turns.user: Remind me how relief drivers fit into the hours-of-service rules.
turns.reply: Briefing section 7 covers driver scheduling: a relief driver may take over only at a listed layover point, and the incoming driver's hours-of-service clock must show enough headroom to finish the leg. The useful detail is that relief handoffs inside a depot do not pause the service window. (Section 7.)

turns.user: What is the claim window after a near-miss is reported?
turns.reply: Briefing section 8 covers incident reporting: a near-miss form opens a ten-day claim window, and escalation past the depot lead follows the published escalation tree. One more detail: the window is measured from the incident time, not the filing time, so late filings shorten it. (Section 8.)
